content:
Quick Verdict Choosing between Beehiiv and Substack? Here’s the short version: Substack is the simpler choice – start writing immediately, tap into a built-in audience network, and pay nothing until you monetize (then 10% of revenue). Beehiiv is the growth-focused choice – more monetization options, advanced analytics, and full control over your brand, with flat monthly pricing that doesn’t scale with your revenue. The right pick depends on whether you prioritize simplicity or control.
At a Glance Feature Beehiiv Substack Free plan Yes (up to 2,500 subscribers) Yes (unlimited subscribers) Paid plans $49/mo (Scale), $109/mo (Max) Free (10% revenue share) Revenue cut 0% on all plans 10% of paid subscription revenue Ad monetization Native ad network built in No native ad support Audience network Recommendations + Boosts marketplace Notes + Recommendations Custom domain Yes (all plans) Yes (all plans) Analytics Advanced (UTM, segments, funnels) Basic (opens, clicks, subs) Best for Newsletter businesses Writers & essayists What Is Beehiiv? Beehiiv launched in 2022, founded by early Morning Brew employees who understood exactly what growth-focused newsletter operators need. The platform was built from the ground up for creators who treat their newsletter as a business, not just a writing hobby.
The standout feature is monetization flexibility. Unlike Substack’s single revenue model (paid subscriptions), Beehiiv offers multiple paths to income. The native Ad Network connects you with brand sponsors – you can monetize your free newsletter without ever charging readers. You can also sell digital products (PDFs, templates, courses) directly through the platform with zero commission from Beehiiv. And yes, you can run paid subscriptions too, keeping 100% of the revenue (minus payment processing fees).
Growth tools are where Beehiiv really separates itself. Boosts is a paid recommendation marketplace where you can acquire subscribers by paying other newsletter creators to recommend you (or earn money by recommending others). The referral program lets readers unlock rewards for sharing your newsletter. Add in advanced segmentation, A/B testing, automation workflows, and detailed analytics with UTM tracking, and you have a platform that’s built for scale. The Scale plan starts at $49/month for up to 1,000 subscribers (pricing increases with subscriber count), while the Max plan at $109/month adds white-labeling, priority support, and advanced AI tools.
What Is Substack? Substack is the platform that kickstarted the modern newsletter renaissance. Since 2017, it has attracted some of the biggest names in journalism, politics, and culture by offering the simplest possible deal: write, publish, and we’ll handle everything else.
The zero-friction model is Substack’s greatest strength. There are no plans to choose, no features to configure, no upfront costs. You sign up, start writing, and publish. If you want to charge readers for subscriptions, Substack takes a 10% cut plus Stripe processing fees (roughly 2.9% + $0.30 per transaction). If you never charge, you never pay anything.
Substack’s network effects are genuinely powerful. The platform has evolved beyond email into a social reading experience with Notes (a Twitter-like feed for short posts), Chat (community discussions), and Recommendations (cross-promotion between writers). When established Substack writers recommend your newsletter, you can gain hundreds of subscribers overnight. The platform also hosts podcasts and video, making it a multi-format publishing tool. Substack’s reader app has millions of active users browsing and discovering new writers – a built-in audience that no other newsletter platform can match.
Head-to-Head Comparison Pricing This is where the comparison gets nuanced. Substack appears free, but the 10% revenue share adds up fast.
Example: A newsletter with 5,000 paid subscribers at $10/month generates $50,000/month. Substack takes $5,000/month ($60,000/year). On Beehiiv, you’d pay a flat monthly fee based on subscriber count – roughly $99-$159/month for that tier, keeping over $58,000 more per year.
At lower volumes, Substack wins. If you have 100 paid subscribers at $5/month, Substack takes $50/month. Beehiiv’s Scale plan costs $49/month regardless. The crossover point is roughly when your paid subscription revenue exceeds $500-600/month.
Features Beehiiv wins on: analytics (detailed UTM tracking, subscriber scoring, engagement funnels), monetization options (ads, digital products, Boosts), design customization, automation workflows, and A/B testing.
Substack wins on: simplicity, built-in audience network (Notes, reader app), community features (Chat, discussion threads), multi-format publishing (podcasts + video), and discoverability through the Substack network.
Ease of Use Substack wins decisively here. The editor is distraction-free, there are no settings to configure, and publishing takes seconds. It feels like writing in a journal.
Beehiiv is more capable but requires more setup. You’ll spend time configuring segments, designing templates, setting up automations, and learning the analytics dashboard. It’s not difficult, but there’s a real onboarding period before you’re using the platform to its potential.
Who Should Use What? Pick Beehiiv if: you’re building a newsletter business and want maximum control over monetization, branding, and growth. You plan to monetize through ads and sponsorships (not just paid subscriptions). Your revenue will exceed $500/month from subscriptions, making Substack’s 10% cut more expensive than Beehiiv’s flat fee.
Pick Substack if: you’re a writer first and a business operator second. You value simplicity and want to focus on writing, not platform configuration. You want access to Substack’s built-in audience network for discoverability. You’re just starting out and don’t want to pay anything until you have paying readers.
FAQ Does Substack really take 10% forever? Yes. Unlike a SaaS fee that stays flat, Substack’s 10% scales with your revenue indefinitely. At $1,000/month in subscription revenue, you pay $100. At $10,000/month, you pay $1,000. There’s no cap, no volume discount, and no way to negotiate it down.
Can I switch from Substack to Beehiiv? Yes, and Beehiiv makes this easy with a direct Substack import tool. You can migrate your subscriber list, archive posts, and even preserve your custom domain. Many high-profile creators have made this switch as their revenue grew.
Which is better for free newsletters? Both work well. Substack’s network effects give free newsletters better organic discovery. Beehiiv’s Ad Network lets free newsletters monetize through sponsorships without ever charging readers – a revenue path Substack doesn’t offer.
Can I use Beehiiv’s ad network from day one? You need to be on the Scale plan ($49/month) or higher and meet minimum subscriber thresholds to access the ad network. Most new creators start with the free Launch plan and upgrade once they’ve built an initial audience.
Our Recommendation If you’re just starting a newsletter and want zero risk, Substack is the obvious choice. You pay nothing, the writing experience is beautiful, and the network can accelerate early growth.
Once your newsletter becomes a business – especially if subscription revenue passes $500/month or you want to monetize through ads – Beehiiv becomes the smarter financial choice. The flat pricing model, multiple revenue streams, and advanced growth tools are built for creators who think of their newsletter as a company.
Many successful creators start on Substack for the simplicity and network, then migrate to Beehiiv when they outgrow it. That’s a perfectly valid strategy.

content:
Quick Verdict Choosing between Canva and Adobe Express? Here’s the short version: Canva wins for most people thanks to its massive template library, intuitive drag-and-drop editor, and generous free tier. Adobe Express is the better pick if you already pay for Adobe Creative Cloud or need tight integration with Photoshop and Illustrator. Both are capable tools, but Canva’s head start in the browser-based design space still shows.
At a Glance Feature Canva Adobe Express Pricing Free / $12.99/mo (Pro) Free / $9.99/mo (Premium) Free tier Yes — 1.6M+ templates, 5GB storage Yes — limited templates, 25 AI credits/mo Templates 1.6M+ 200K+ AI features Magic Write, Magic Eraser, text-to-image Firefly generative AI (250 credits/mo on Premium) Cloud storage 5GB free / 1TB Pro 2GB free / 100GB Premium Best for Non-designers, social media managers, small businesses Adobe ecosystem users, brand-focused teams What Is Canva? Canva launched in 2013 and has grown into the default design tool for people who are not professional designers. It runs entirely in the browser (plus mobile apps) and covers everything from Instagram posts and presentations to resumes and video editing.
What makes Canva sticky is the sheer volume of ready-made content. With over 1.6 million templates and 4.7 million stock photos, icons, and illustrations available on the free plan alone, most users never need to start from a blank canvas. The drag-and-drop editor is genuinely easy to pick up — most people are productive within minutes.
Canva has pushed hard into AI with its Magic Studio suite. Magic Write generates copy, Magic Eraser removes backgrounds and objects, and the text-to-image tool creates visuals from prompts. These features are bundled into the Pro plan, which also unlocks a Brand Kit for consistent colors, fonts, and logos across designs.
What Is Adobe Express? Adobe Express is Adobe’s answer to Canva — a simplified, web-based design tool aimed at non-designers and social media creators. It was rebuilt from scratch in 2022 (replacing Adobe Spark) and leans heavily on Adobe’s Firefly generative AI for image generation, text effects, and template creation.
The main draw of Adobe Express is its connection to the broader Adobe ecosystem. Premium subscribers get access to over 200 million Adobe Stock assets, and designs can be opened directly in Photoshop or Illustrator for advanced editing. If your team already uses Creative Cloud, Express fits naturally into that workflow.
Adobe Express also benefits from Firefly’s commercially safe AI — every AI-generated asset comes with content credentials, which matters for brands worried about copyright liability. The free tier is more limited than Canva’s, capping generative AI credits at 25 per month and imposing a 300MB file upload limit.
Head-to-Head Comparison Pricing Canva Pro costs $12.99/month (or $119.99/year). The free tier is remarkably generous and many casual users never need to upgrade.
Adobe Express Premium costs $9.99/month (or $99.99/year). On paper it is cheaper, but the free tier is significantly more restricted than Canva’s. Adobe Express is also included free with any Creative Cloud subscription, which makes it effectively a bonus for existing Adobe customers.
Plan Canva Adobe Express Free $0 — 5GB storage, 1.6M+ templates $0 — 2GB storage, 25 AI credits Individual $12.99/mo $9.99/mo Teams $14.99/mo per user (up to 5) Custom pricing Enterprise Custom Custom Features Templates and assets: Canva has the edge with 1.6M+ templates versus Adobe Express’s smaller library. However, Adobe Express gives Premium users access to 200M+ Adobe Stock assets, which is hard to beat for photo-heavy projects.
AI tools: Both offer generative AI. Canva’s Magic Studio covers text generation, image generation, background removal, and object erasing. Adobe’s Firefly focuses on image generation and text effects, with the advantage of built-in content credentials for commercial safety.
Video editing: Canva has pulled ahead here with a surprisingly capable timeline-based video editor. Adobe Express offers basic video editing but pushes serious video work to Premiere Pro.
Brand management: Both offer Brand Kits on paid plans. Canva allows one brand on Pro; Adobe Express ties brand assets to Creative Cloud Libraries for cross-app consistency.
Ease of Use Canva is easier to learn. The interface is clean, tutorials are built-in, and the template-first approach means you are rarely staring at a blank page. Most users are comfortable within 10-15 minutes.
Adobe Express has improved significantly since its Spark days, but the interface still carries some Adobe complexity. If you have never used an Adobe product, expect a slightly steeper learning curve. If you are already in the Adobe ecosystem, it will feel familiar and intuitive.
Who Should Use What? Pick Canva if: you are a solo creator, social media manager, small business owner, or anyone who needs to produce professional-looking graphics quickly without design training. The free tier alone is enough for most casual users.
Pick Adobe Express if: you already pay for Adobe Creative Cloud and want a quick-design tool that integrates with Photoshop, Illustrator, and your existing asset libraries. It is also worth considering if AI content credentials matter to your brand’s compliance requirements.
FAQ Is Canva better than Adobe Express? For most users, yes. Canva has more templates, a more intuitive editor, and a far more generous free tier. Adobe Express is better specifically for users already invested in the Adobe ecosystem or those who need Firefly’s commercially safe AI generation.
Can I use Canva and Adobe Express for free? Both offer free plans. Canva’s free tier is one of the most generous in the design space — 1.6M+ templates, 5GB storage, and basic AI features. Adobe Express’s free tier is more limited, with only 25 AI credits per month and a 300MB upload cap.
Do either of them replace Photoshop? No. Both are designed for quick, template-based work — social posts, presentations, flyers, and simple video. For photo retouching, compositing, or print production, you still need Photoshop or a comparable tool.
Can I switch from one to the other? Both support PNG, JPG, and PDF export, so you can move final assets freely. Neither imports the other’s native project files, so you would need to rebuild editable designs from scratch.
Our Recommendation For most readers, Canva is the better choice. The template library is larger, the editor is more intuitive, and the free plan is generous enough that many users never need to pay. Start with Canva Free and upgrade to Pro only when you need Brand Kit, background removal, or premium templates.
Adobe Express makes sense if you are already paying for Creative Cloud — it is included free and integrates cleanly with Photoshop and Illustrator. Do not pay for it standalone unless you specifically need Firefly’s content credentials.
Try Canva free → | Try Adobe Express free →

content:
Quick Verdict Choosing between ChatGPT Plus and Claude Pro? Here’s the short version: ChatGPT Plus is the Swiss Army knife — broader tool integrations, image generation, web browsing, and a massive plugin ecosystem. Claude Pro is the specialist for deep work — longer context windows, better handling of large documents, and more nuanced reasoning on complex tasks. Both cost $20/month. Your choice depends on whether you need breadth or depth.
At a Glance Feature ChatGPT Plus Claude Pro Pricing $20/mo $20/mo Free tier Yes (GPT-5.4 with limits) Yes (Claude with limits) Top model GPT-5.3 / GPT-5.4 Thinking Claude Opus 4 / Sonnet 4 Context window 128K tokens Up to 1M tokens Image generation Yes (DALL-E 3) No Web browsing Yes Yes (Research mode) Code execution Yes (sandbox) Yes (sandbox) Best for Broad tool use, plugins, image generation Long documents, nuanced writing, coding What Is ChatGPT Plus? ChatGPT Plus is OpenAI’s premium consumer tier at $20/month. It gives you access to GPT-5, the most broadly capable language model available, along with a suite of integrated tools that no competitor fully matches.
The feature set is wide. Plus subscribers get DALL-E 3 image generation, web browsing, code execution in a sandboxed environment, file upload and analysis, custom GPTs (specialized chatbots you can build or install from a marketplace), and task automation features. The model picker lets you switch between GPT-5.3 Instant (fast) and GPT-5.4 Thinking (deeper reasoning) depending on task complexity.
OpenAI has also introduced a broader plan lineup: Free, Go ($8/month), Plus ($20/month), and Pro ($200/month). Plus remains the sweet spot for most power users. You get up to 160 GPT-5.3 messages every 3 hours and up to 3,000 GPT-5.4 Thinking messages per week — generous limits that rarely feel constraining for individual use.
What Is Claude Pro? Claude Pro is Anthropic’s premium tier at $20/month (or $17/month on an annual plan). It provides 5x the usage of the free tier and access to Claude’s full model lineup: Opus (most capable), Sonnet (balanced), and Haiku (fast).
Where Claude stands out is depth. Claude supports context windows up to 1 million tokens — roughly 700,000 words — which means you can paste entire codebases, legal contracts, research papers, or book manuscripts into a single conversation. No other consumer AI product handles this volume of input as well.
Claude Pro also includes Claude Code (a terminal-based coding agent), Cowork (a collaborative mode where Claude works alongside you), Research mode for deep web research, unlimited projects for organizing documents and chats, Google Workspace integration, and cross-conversation memory. Anthropic expanded the Pro plan significantly in early 2026, making it considerably more capable than the original launch version.
Head-to-Head Comparison Pricing Both plans cost $20/month, making this one of the most directly comparable matchups in AI.
Feature ChatGPT Plus Claude Pro Monthly price $20 $20 ($17/mo annual) Cheaper tier Go at $8/mo Free only Premium tier Pro at $200/mo Max at $100-$200/mo Free tier limits GPT-5.4 with low caps Claude Sonnet with low caps Claude Pro edges ahead on pure price with the annual billing discount. ChatGPT offers a cheaper entry point with the Go plan at $8/month for users who do not need full Plus features.
Reasoning and Writing Quality ChatGPT Plus (GPT-5.4 Thinking) is strong at structured reasoning, math, and code generation. It handles multi-step problems well and the Thinking mode provides deeper analysis when you need it.
Claude Pro (Opus) excels at nuanced writing, literary analysis, and tasks that require holding large amounts of context simultaneously. It tends to produce more natural-sounding prose and is less likely to fall into formulaic patterns. For editing, summarization, and working with long documents, Claude has a measurable edge.
Both are excellent at coding. ChatGPT has the edge in its code execution sandbox (you can run Python directly). Claude Code offers a terminal-based agent that can read, write, and modify files across entire projects.
Tool Ecosystem ChatGPT Plus wins on breadth. DALL-E 3 image generation, web browsing, file analysis, custom GPTs, the plugin marketplace, and task automation give it the widest feature set of any consumer AI.
Claude Pro is more focused. No image generation, but strong web research, Google Workspace integration, MCP connectors for third-party tools, and a project system for persistent context. Claude Code is a unique strength — a genuine coding agent that operates in your terminal.
Context and Document Handling This is Claude’s strongest advantage. The 1M token context window means you can upload a 300-page PDF and ask questions about page 247 without the model losing track. ChatGPT’s 128K token window is large by industry standards but cannot match this for truly large documents.
If you work with long contracts, research papers, codebases, or manuscripts, this difference is not marginal — it is the deciding factor.
Ease of Use Both have clean, modern web interfaces. ChatGPT’s is more feature-dense (model picker, GPT store, image generation inline). Claude’s is more minimalist and text-focused.
ChatGPT’s mobile app is more mature with voice mode and camera input. Claude’s mobile app covers the basics but has fewer multimedia features.
Who Should Use What? Pick ChatGPT Plus if: you want the broadest set of AI capabilities in one subscription. Image generation, web browsing, code execution, plugins, and custom GPTs make it the most versatile option. It is the better choice for users who want a single tool that does everything reasonably well.
Pick Claude Pro if: you work with long documents, complex writing, or large codebases. The 1M token context window is a genuine competitive advantage that matters for legal work, research, software engineering, and academic writing. Claude Code is also the better option for developers who want an AI coding assistant that works directly in their terminal.
FAQ Which one is smarter? It depends on the task. GPT-5.4 Thinking and Claude Opus are both frontier models. GPT-5.4 tends to score higher on math and structured reasoning benchmarks. Claude Opus tends to perform better on writing quality, nuance, and tasks requiring large-context understanding. For everyday use, both are excellent.
Can I use both? Yes, and many power users do. A common setup is ChatGPT Plus for quick tasks, image generation, and web browsing, with Claude Pro for deep writing, document analysis, and coding projects. At $40/month total, it covers virtually every AI use case.
Do they remember previous conversations? ChatGPT has a memory feature that persists facts across conversations (you can view and edit what it remembers). Claude recently added cross-conversation memory for Pro users, though it is newer and less mature than ChatGPT’s implementation.
What about the API? Both offer developer APIs priced separately from consumer subscriptions. OpenAI’s API uses GPT-5 models; Anthropic’s API uses Claude models. API pricing is per-token and does not require a Plus or Pro subscription.
Our Recommendation If you can only pick one, ChatGPT Plus is the safer default for most people. The breadth of features — image generation, browsing, plugins, code execution — makes it the more complete daily driver.
If you work primarily with text — writing, editing, analyzing documents, coding — Claude Pro is the better tool. The context window advantage is real, the writing quality is best-in-class, and Claude Code is a genuinely useful coding agent.
If you can afford $40/month, use both. They complement each other well.

content:
Quick Verdict Choosing between Cursor and GitHub Copilot? Here’s the short version: GitHub Copilot is the better value for most developers – solid autocomplete, agent mode, and deep GitHub integration at $10/month. Cursor is the more powerful AI coding environment for developers who want full codebase-aware AI assistance and are willing to pay $20/month for it. Copilot augments your editor; Cursor replaces it.
At a Glance Feature Cursor GitHub Copilot Free plan Yes (limited requests) Yes (2,000 completions + 50 chats/mo) Pro price $20/mo $10/mo (Pro) Premium tier $200/mo (Ultra) $39/mo (Pro+) Architecture Standalone editor (VS Code fork) Extension for VS Code, JetBrains, etc. Agent mode Yes (Composer) Yes (Agent Mode) Model access GPT-4o, Claude, Gemini + more GPT-4o, Claude Sonnet, Gemini 2.5 Pro Codebase indexing Full project indexing Repository-level context Best for AI-native coding workflows Everyday code assistance What Is Cursor? Cursor is a standalone AI code editor built by Anysphere, a company that raised $2.3 billion at a $29.3 billion valuation in late 2025 – numbers that signal how seriously the industry takes AI-native development tools. Cursor is a fork of VS Code, meaning it looks and feels familiar to the millions of developers already using Microsoft’s editor, but with AI deeply integrated into every interaction.
The key differentiator is Composer – Cursor’s agent mode. Unlike inline code suggestions, Composer plans and executes multi-file changes across your entire codebase. Describe a feature in natural language, and Composer will create files, modify existing code, run terminal commands, and iterate until the task is complete. Cursor indexes your entire codebase, giving the AI genuine context about how your code fits together.
In mid-2025, Cursor switched to a credit-based system. Pro ($20/month) includes a $20 credit pool that depletes based on which AI models you use. Ultra ($200/month) is for power users running intensive agent tasks. Auto mode provides unlimited usage on all paid plans. Teams ($40/user/month) and Enterprise (custom) add collaboration features.
What Is GitHub Copilot? GitHub Copilot is Microsoft’s AI coding assistant, deeply integrated with the GitHub ecosystem that most developers already use. Rather than replacing your editor, Copilot lives inside it – available as an extension for VS Code, JetBrains IDEs, Neovim, and more. This approach means zero workflow disruption: you keep your editor, your extensions, your keybindings.
Copilot’s autocomplete remains its bread and butter. As you type, it suggests entire lines or blocks of code based on context. The suggestions are fast, surprisingly accurate, and get better as it learns your patterns. But Copilot has grown well beyond autocomplete. Agent Mode (launched in 2025) lets Copilot autonomously plan multi-step coding tasks, create branches, open pull requests, and even respond to code review comments – all integrated with GitHub’s pull request workflow.
The pricing is straightforward: a Free tier offers 2,000 completions and 50 chat messages per month. Pro at $10/month gives 300 premium model requests with unlimited completions. Pro+ at $39/month unlocks 1,500 premium requests and access to top-tier models like Claude Opus 4 and OpenAI o3. Business ($19/user/month) and Enterprise ($39/user/month) add organization-wide management, policy controls, and knowledge bases.
Head-to-Head Comparison Pricing GitHub Copilot Pro costs $10/month – half of Cursor Pro’s $20/month. At the premium tier, Copilot Pro+ ($39/month) is also cheaper than Cursor’s Ultra ($200/month), though they serve different intensity levels.
For teams, Copilot Business at $19/user/month undercuts Cursor Teams at $40/user/month significantly. Both offer annual billing discounts (Copilot saves ~17%, Cursor saves ~20%).
Value assessment: Copilot gives you 80% of the utility at 50% of the price. Cursor’s premium buys you deeper codebase understanding and more powerful multi-file editing.
Features Cursor wins on: multi-file refactoring, full codebase indexing, the Composer agent for complex cross-file changes, and the overall “AI-native” editing experience. When you need to understand and modify an unfamiliar codebase, Cursor’s deep indexing is a genuine advantage.
Copilot wins on: ecosystem integration (GitHub PRs, issues, Actions), editor flexibility (works in VS Code, JetBrains, Neovim), autocomplete speed and accuracy, and the new Agent Mode’s ability to create branches and PRs directly. On SWE-bench, Copilot solved 56% of tasks versus Cursor’s 51.7%.
Ease of Use Copilot is easier to adopt. Install the extension, sign in, and start coding. Your existing editor setup stays exactly as it is. The autocomplete is unobtrusive – accept suggestions with Tab, ignore them by typing.
Cursor requires switching to a new editor. While it imports your VS Code settings and extensions, it’s still a context switch. The Composer agent and codebase indexing features take time to learn and configure effectively. Once you’re proficient, the workflow is more powerful, but the ramp-up is real.
Who Should Use What? Pick Cursor if: you regularly work on large, unfamiliar codebases and need AI that truly understands your full project structure. You do a lot of multi-file refactoring. You want an AI-first editing experience and don’t mind paying $20/month for it. You’re building features that touch the entire stack.
Pick GitHub Copilot if: you want reliable, fast code suggestions without changing your editor or workflow. You use GitHub heavily and want AI that integrates with PRs, issues, and Actions. Budget matters – $10/month for Pro is excellent value. You primarily write new code and fix bugs inline rather than doing large-scale refactoring.
FAQ Can I use Cursor and GitHub Copilot together? Technically, you could install the Copilot extension inside Cursor (since it’s a VS Code fork), but it’s redundant and can create conflicts. Pick one.
Is Cursor just VS Code with AI? It started that way, but Cursor has diverged significantly. The codebase indexing, Composer agent, and credit-based model system are proprietary features that go well beyond what any VS Code extension can do. It shares VS Code’s interface and extension ecosystem but is a meaningfully different product.
Which is better for beginners? GitHub Copilot. The inline suggestions are easier to understand and accept/reject. Cursor’s more advanced features (Composer, multi-file edits) assume familiarity with software architecture concepts that newer developers may not have yet.
Does Cursor work with GitHub? Yes. Cursor has full Git integration and works with GitHub repositories the same way VS Code does. You just don’t get Copilot’s native GitHub PR/Issue integration features.
Our Recommendation For most developers, GitHub Copilot Pro at $10/month is the right starting point. It’s half the price, works in your existing editor, and the autocomplete + Agent Mode cover the vast majority of daily coding needs. The free tier is also generous enough to test whether AI coding assistance fits your workflow.
If you’re a senior developer or team lead who regularly navigates large codebases, refactors across many files, or builds complex features from scratch, Cursor Pro at $20/month earns its premium. The depth of codebase understanding and Composer’s multi-file capabilities are genuinely ahead of what Copilot offers in those scenarios.

content:
Quick Verdict Choosing between Grammarly and ProWritingAid? Here’s the short version: Grammarly is the faster, more polished tool for everyday writing — emails, documents, and quick edits. ProWritingAid goes deeper with 25+ writing analysis reports that help long-form writers actually improve their craft. Grammarly is the better daily driver; ProWritingAid is the better writing coach.
At a Glance Feature Grammarly ProWritingAid Pricing Free / $12/mo (annual) Free / $10/mo (annual) Monthly billing $30/mo $30/mo Lifetime option No Yes — $399 one-time Free tier Basic grammar and spelling 500-word limit per check Writing reports Limited (tone, clarity) 25+ detailed reports Best for Everyday writing and email Long-form writers and authors What Is Grammarly? Grammarly is the most widely used writing assistant, with over 30 million daily active users. It works as a browser extension, desktop app, keyboard on mobile, and integrates directly into Google Docs, Microsoft Word, and most text fields on the web.
At its core, Grammarly catches grammar, spelling, and punctuation errors in real time. The Pro plan (formerly called Premium) adds advanced suggestions for clarity, tone, word choice, and full-sentence rewrites. It also includes a plagiarism checker that scans against billions of web pages — useful for students and content creators.
In 2025, Grammarly consolidated its pricing by merging the old Premium and Business tiers into a single “Pro” plan. Pro now supports teams of up to 149 people under one account, includes AI-powered tone detection, and offers style guide customization. The AI writing assistant can also generate drafts, rewrite paragraphs, and adjust formality levels on demand.
What Is ProWritingAid? ProWritingAid positions itself as a writing mentor rather than just a grammar checker. While it catches the same grammar and spelling errors as Grammarly, its real strength is the 25+ writing analysis reports that break down your text by readability, sentence structure, pacing, dialogue tags, cliches, sticky sentences, and more.
These reports make ProWritingAid particularly popular with novelists, academic writers, and anyone working on manuscripts. You can run a full document analysis and get a visual breakdown of where your writing is strong and where it drags. No other tool at this price point offers that level of structural feedback.
ProWritingAid also stands out with its lifetime license at $399 — a one-time payment that eliminates recurring costs. For writers who know they will use the tool for years, the math works out quickly. The tool integrates with Scrivener (a favorite among fiction writers), Google Docs, Microsoft Word, and most browsers.
Head-to-Head Comparison Pricing Both tools cost $30/month on monthly billing, but annual plans bring the price down significantly.
Plan Grammarly ProWritingAid Monthly $30/mo $30/mo Quarterly $20/mo ($60 total) — Annual $12/mo ($144 total) $10/mo ($120 total) Lifetime Not available $399 one-time Enterprise Custom pricing — ProWritingAid is cheaper on annual billing and the lifetime deal is a genuine differentiator. Grammarly does not offer a lifetime option.
Features Grammar and spelling: Both are excellent. Grammarly is marginally faster at catching errors in real time. ProWritingAid occasionally flags more nuanced style issues.
AI writing assistance: Grammarly’s AI can generate text, rewrite sentences, and adjust tone. ProWritingAid offers “AI Sparks” — shorter AI-powered suggestions — with 3-5 per day depending on your plan.
Writing reports: This is where ProWritingAid dominates. The 25+ reports cover readability, sentence length variation, overused words, passive voice frequency, dialogue analysis, and pacing. Grammarly offers a performance score and basic tone detection, but nothing this comprehensive.
Plagiarism checking: Grammarly includes plagiarism checking on Pro. ProWritingAid offers it as part of Premium Pro (the $36/month tier).
Integrations: Grammarly integrates with more platforms out of the box, including Slack, Discord, and Notion. ProWritingAid has the critical ones — browser, Word, Google Docs — and uniquely supports Scrivener.
Ease of Use Grammarly wins on polish. The interface is clean, suggestions appear inline with one-click acceptance, and the browser extension works seamlessly across the web. Setup takes under two minutes.
ProWritingAid’s interface is functional but busier. The writing reports require a dedicated editor window rather than working inline. There is a learning curve to understanding what each report means and how to act on it — but that depth is exactly what serious writers want.
Who Should Use What? Pick Grammarly if: you write emails, reports, social media posts, or any everyday business content and want a tool that catches errors fast without getting in your way. The browser extension alone justifies the price for professionals who write across multiple platforms daily.
Pick ProWritingAid if: you write novels, academic papers, blog posts, or any long-form content where structural quality matters. The writing reports will genuinely help you improve as a writer over time, and the lifetime deal makes it the better long-term investment.
FAQ Is Grammarly more accurate than ProWritingAid? For basic grammar and spelling, both are very accurate. Grammarly tends to catch errors faster in real-time editing. ProWritingAid sometimes surfaces style issues that Grammarly misses, particularly around sentence structure and readability.
Does ProWritingAid work with Scrivener? Yes — ProWritingAid is one of the few writing tools with a native Scrivener integration. This is a major draw for fiction writers who use Scrivener for manuscript organization.
Can I use both? You can, and some writers do. A common approach is using Grammarly’s browser extension for daily emails and short writing, while running longer pieces through ProWritingAid’s detailed reports. That said, paying for both gets expensive unless you use ProWritingAid’s lifetime deal.
Is the ProWritingAid lifetime deal worth it? At $399, the lifetime deal pays for itself in about 3.3 years versus the annual plan ($120/year). If you plan to write seriously for more than three years, it is a strong value.
Our Recommendation For most professionals and casual writers, Grammarly is the better daily tool. It is faster, more polished, and its browser extension works everywhere you type. The $12/month annual price is fair for the productivity gains.
For serious writers — novelists, academics, content creators who care about craft — ProWritingAid is the better investment. The writing reports provide feedback you cannot get anywhere else at this price, and the lifetime deal eliminates ongoing costs entirely.

Quick Verdict Choosing between Jasper and Copy.ai? Here’s the short version: Jasper is the better pick for marketing teams that need airtight brand consistency across long-form content. Copy.ai wins for solopreneurs, freelancers, and small teams who want unlimited AI writing without the premium price tag. Both tools have matured significantly, but they’ve diverged in focus – Jasper doubled down on brand voice, while Copy.ai expanded into workflow automation.
At a Glance Feature Jasper Copy.ai Starting price $39/mo (Creator) $29/mo (Chat) Pro/main plan $69/mo (Pro) $29/mo (Chat) Free tier 7-day trial only Limited free plan available Word limit Unlimited on all paid plans Unlimited on paid plans Brand voice Advanced (learns from your examples) Basic brand voice settings Templates 50+ marketing-focused 90+ short-form templates Best for Marketing teams & agencies Solopreneurs & small teams Workflow automation Limited Yes (Agents plan at $249/mo) What Is Jasper? Jasper is an AI writing platform built specifically for marketing teams. Originally launched as Jarvis in 2021, it quickly became one of the highest-grossing AI writing tools by focusing on a specific pain point: helping marketers produce on-brand content at scale.
What sets Jasper apart is its Brand Voice engine. You feed it your style guides, past content, and tone preferences, and it learns to write like your brand. This isn’t just a toggle – it genuinely adapts output to match your company’s voice across blog posts, emails, ad copy, and social media. For agencies managing multiple clients, you can set up separate brand voices for each account.
Jasper also offers a collaborative Canvas editor (similar to Google Docs but with AI baked in), SEO mode for blog optimization, and integrations with tools like Surfer SEO and Grammarly. The platform supports multiple AI models and recently added image generation capabilities. The Business plan adds API access, custom workflows, and dedicated account management for larger organizations.
What Is Copy.ai? Copy.ai started as a short-form copywriting tool – think ad headlines, product descriptions, and social media captions – but has evolved into something broader. In 2025-2026, the platform pivoted hard toward AI-powered workflow automation, positioning itself as more than just a writing assistant.
The core writing experience remains strong with over 90 templates covering everything from Instagram captions to cold email sequences. The interface is clean and beginner-friendly: pick a template, fill in the brief, and get multiple variations in seconds. There’s no steep learning curve, which is a genuine advantage over Jasper’s more feature-heavy setup.
Where Copy.ai gets interesting is its newer Agents plan ($249/month). This lets you build AI-powered workflows that go beyond writing – think automated content pipelines, research agents, and multi-step marketing tasks. It’s a different bet than Jasper: instead of perfecting the writing itself, Copy.ai is betting that teams want AI to handle entire workflows. The Chat plan at $29/month gives you access to multiple AI models (OpenAI, Anthropic, Gemini) with unlimited words and 5 team seats included.
Head-to-Head Comparison Pricing Jasper’s Creator plan starts at $39/month (single user) and the Pro plan runs $69/month (or $59/month billed annually). The Business plan is custom-priced. Annual billing saves roughly 20%.
Copy.ai’s Chat plan costs $29/month ($24/month billed annually) and includes 5 seats – a significant value difference for small teams. The Agents plan for workflow automation jumps to $249/month with 10 seats and 10,000 workflow credits.
Bottom line: Copy.ai is meaningfully cheaper for most users. A team of 3 people would pay $69/month on Jasper Pro vs. $29/month on Copy.ai Chat.
Features Jasper wins on content quality and brand consistency. Its brand voice feature is genuinely best-in-class for maintaining a unified tone across all marketing output. The Canvas editor is excellent for long-form work, and SEO mode helps optimize blog content for search.
Copy.ai wins on template breadth and automation. With 90+ templates vs. Jasper’s 50+, it covers more short-form use cases out of the box. The workflow automation features (on the Agents plan) are unique – Jasper has nothing comparable.
Ease of Use Copy.ai wins here. The interface is intuitive from the moment you sign up – pick a template, enter your inputs, get results. Most users are productive within minutes.
Jasper has a steeper learning curve. Setting up brand voices, navigating the Canvas editor, and configuring campaigns takes time. The payoff is better output, but you’ll invest more upfront to get there.
Who Should Use What? Pick Jasper if: you’re a marketing team or agency producing high volumes of long-form content and brand consistency is non-negotiable. You’re willing to pay more for higher-quality, on-brand output and you’ll use the brand voice features extensively.
Pick Copy.ai if: you’re a solopreneur, freelancer, or small team that needs solid AI writing at a lower price point. You value simplicity, want multiple team seats included, or you’re interested in building AI-powered content workflows beyond just writing.
FAQ Is Jasper better than Copy.ai for blog writing? Yes, generally. Jasper’s brand voice engine and long-form Canvas editor produce more polished, consistent blog content. Copy.ai is better for shorter pieces like ad copy, emails, and social posts where speed matters more than brand nuance.
Can I use both Jasper and Copy.ai? Absolutely. Some teams use Jasper for long-form blog and website content while using Copy.ai for quick ad copy and social media posts. There’s no data lock-in with either platform.
Does Copy.ai have a free plan? Yes. Copy.ai offers a limited free tier, though the word limits are restrictive. Jasper only offers a 7-day free trial of the Pro plan with no ongoing free tier.
Which has better AI models? Both platforms use top-tier models. Copy.ai gives you access to OpenAI, Anthropic, and Gemini models on the Chat plan. Jasper uses a mix of models optimized for marketing content. In practice, the quality difference comes more from each platform’s fine-tuning and brand voice features than the underlying models.
Our Recommendation For most individual creators and small teams, Copy.ai is the better value. At $29/month with 5 seats and unlimited words, it’s hard to beat on price. The writing quality is solid for everyday marketing content.
If you’re running a marketing team or agency where brand consistency directly impacts revenue, Jasper justifies its premium. The brand voice engine genuinely works and saves hours of editing to maintain tone across a content calendar.

Quick Verdict Choosing between Midjourney and DALL-E 3? Here’s the short version: Midjourney produces more artistic, stylized images with better aesthetic consistency — it is the tool professional creatives reach for. DALL-E 3 is more accessible, better at following complex prompts accurately, and comes bundled with ChatGPT Plus. If image quality and style matter most, go Midjourney. If convenience and prompt accuracy matter more, DALL-E 3.
At a Glance Feature Midjourney DALL-E 3 Pricing $10/mo (Basic) — $120/mo (Mega) Included with ChatGPT Plus ($20/mo) Free tier No Yes (limited, via free ChatGPT) Access method Web app + Discord ChatGPT interface or API Image style Artistic, stylized, cinematic Clean, accurate, literal Best for Creative professionals, artists Quick mockups, accurate illustrations What Is Midjourney? Midjourney is an independent AI image generator that has built a reputation for producing the most visually striking AI art available. Founded by David Holz (co-founder of Leap Motion), the tool started as a Discord-only bot in 2022 and has since added a web-based editor.
What sets Midjourney apart is its aesthetic sensibility. Images tend to have a cinematic, painterly quality that other generators struggle to match. It excels at atmospheric scenes, character design, concept art, and anything where mood and composition matter. Professional illustrators, game designers, and marketing teams use it for ideation and production work.
Midjourney operates on a subscription model with four tiers. The Basic plan ($10/month) gives you about 200 generations with 3.3 hours of Fast GPU time. The Standard plan ($30/month) is the sweet spot for most users — it includes 15 hours of Fast GPU time plus unlimited generations in Relax mode (slower queue). Pro ($60/month) and Mega ($120/month) add more Fast hours and Stealth Mode for private generations.
What Is DALL-E 3? DALL-E 3 is OpenAI’s image generation model, integrated directly into ChatGPT. Unlike its predecessor, DALL-E 3 was built to work conversationally — you describe what you want in natural language, and ChatGPT refines the prompt behind the scenes before generating the image.
The biggest advantage of DALL-E 3 is prompt adherence. It is remarkably good at including specific details, text, spatial relationships, and complex compositions that you describe. If you type “a red bicycle leaning against a blue wall with a cat sitting in the basket and a sign that says OPEN,” DALL-E 3 will usually nail every element. Midjourney would produce a more beautiful image but might rearrange or drop details.
DALL-E 3 is included with ChatGPT Plus ($20/month), which also gets you GPT-5, web browsing, data analysis, and code interpretation. This makes it strong value if you already want ChatGPT’s other features. Free ChatGPT users also get limited DALL-E 3 access with daily caps. For developers, the API charges $0.04-$0.12 per image depending on quality and resolution.
Head-to-Head Comparison Pricing Plan Midjourney DALL-E 3 Free Not available Limited (via free ChatGPT) Entry $10/mo (Basic — ~200 images) $20/mo (ChatGPT Plus — generous limits) Mid-tier $30/mo (Standard — unlimited Relax) Included in Plus High-tier $60/mo (Pro) / $120/mo (Mega) $200/mo (ChatGPT Pro — highest limits) Annual discount 20% off all plans Not available If you only need image generation, Midjourney Basic at $10/month is cheaper. But if you also use ChatGPT for writing, coding, or research, the $20/month Plus plan bundles DALL-E 3 with everything else — better overall value.
Image Quality Midjourney produces images with stronger composition, lighting, and artistic coherence. Its default aesthetic leans cinematic and polished. It handles styles like watercolor, oil painting, photography, and concept art with impressive consistency.
DALL-E 3 produces cleaner, more literal images. They tend to look more “digital” but are highly accurate to the prompt. Text rendering is significantly better in DALL-E 3 — it can reliably place readable text in images, which Midjourney still struggles with.
Prompt Handling DALL-E 3 wins on prompt accuracy. Because ChatGPT preprocesses your prompt, it expands vague descriptions into detailed specifications. You can also have a conversation to iteratively refine results.
Midjourney requires more prompt engineering skill. Learning parameter syntax (aspect ratios, stylize values, chaos settings) unlocks better results, but there is a genuine learning curve. The Discord community shares prompt techniques actively, which helps.
Ease of Use DALL-E 3 is easier to start with. If you have a ChatGPT account, you just type what you want. No learning curve, no parameters to memorize, no separate platform to sign up for.
Midjourney now offers a web app alongside Discord, which has improved accessibility. But the workflow is still less intuitive than just chatting with ChatGPT. Variation, upscaling, and style tuning require learning the interface.
Who Should Use What? Pick Midjourney if: you are a creative professional, designer, or artist who cares about aesthetic quality and style control. If you are generating images for portfolios, client presentations, game art, or marketing campaigns where visual impact matters, Midjourney is worth the investment. The Standard plan ($30/month) with unlimited Relax mode is the best value.
Pick DALL-E 3 if: you need quick, accurate image generation alongside other AI tools. It is ideal for bloggers who need post illustrations, marketers creating mockups, or anyone who values convenience over artistic perfection. The ChatGPT Plus bundle at $20/month is hard to beat if you use ChatGPT regularly.
FAQ Is Midjourney free? No. As of 2026, Midjourney does not offer a free tier. The cheapest plan is Basic at $10/month ($8/month with annual billing).
Can DALL-E 3 match Midjourney’s image quality? For photorealistic and artistic images, Midjourney still has a clear edge in composition and mood. DALL-E 3 is better at accuracy, text in images, and following complex multi-element prompts. Quality depends on what you are optimizing for.
Can I use the images commercially? Yes, both tools grant commercial usage rights on paid plans. Midjourney requires a paid subscription for commercial use. DALL-E 3 images generated through ChatGPT Plus are also cleared for commercial use under OpenAI’s terms.
What about Stable Diffusion? Stable Diffusion is the open-source alternative — free to run locally if you have a capable GPU. It offers maximum control and customization but requires technical setup. For most users who want a turnkey solution, Midjourney or DALL-E 3 is the better path.
Our Recommendation For creative professionals who need high-quality, stylized imagery, Midjourney Standard ($30/month) is the best tool available right now. The aesthetic quality is unmatched, and unlimited Relax mode means you never run out of generations.
For everyone else — especially if you already use ChatGPT — DALL-E 3 via ChatGPT Plus ($20/month) is the smarter buy. You get solid image generation bundled with the most capable AI assistant on the market. Start there and upgrade to Midjourney only if you need more artistic control.
Try Midjourney → | Try ChatGPT Plus (includes DALL-E 3) →

Quick Verdict Choosing between Notion and Coda? Here’s the short version: Notion is the stronger all-in-one workspace – docs, wikis, project management, and now AI baked into the platform. Coda is the better pick if you want to build powerful custom docs that behave like apps, with deeper automation and formula capabilities. Think of it this way: Notion organizes your work, Coda automates it.
At a Glance Feature Notion Coda Free plan Yes (individual use, 5MB uploads) Yes (unlimited editors, limited doc size) Paid starting price $10/user/mo (Plus, billed annually) $10/doc maker/mo (Pro, billed annually) Business plan $20/user/mo (includes AI) $30/doc maker/mo (Team) AI features Built into Business plan Available via Packs Billing model Per user Per doc maker (viewers free) Integrations 70+ native 600+ via Packs Best for All-in-one workspace Docs that work like apps What Is Notion? Notion is the workspace platform that tried to replace your wiki, project manager, and note-taking app all at once – and largely succeeded. Since launching in 2016, it has grown into one of the most widely adopted productivity tools for both individuals and teams, with a user base that spans startups, enterprises, and solo creators.
The core of Notion is its block-based editor. Every piece of content is a block you can drag, nest, and reconfigure. The same tool can be a meeting notes doc, a product roadmap, a CRM, or a company wiki. Databases are particularly powerful, with multiple views (table, board, calendar, timeline, gallery) that let you slice the same data different ways.
In 2025, Notion eliminated the separate AI add-on and folded AI directly into the Business plan at $20/user/month. Notion AI can summarize pages, generate content, answer questions about your workspace, and autofill database properties. Plus users ($10/user/month) need to upgrade for AI access.
What Is Coda? Coda positions itself as the “doc that grows with your idea” – and that’s not just marketing speak. While it looks like a document editor on the surface, Coda is really a low-code platform disguised as a doc. You can build interactive apps, dashboards, and workflows without ever leaving the document.
The secret sauce is Coda’s formula language and automation engine. Unlike Notion’s relatively simple formulas, Coda’s formula system is closer to a real programming language. You can write logic that triggers actions, pulls data from external services, and updates in real time. Combined with Packs (Coda’s integration system with 600+ connections), you can build docs that sync with Slack, Gmail, Jira, Salesforce, and hundreds of other tools.
Coda’s pricing model is notably different from Notion’s: you only pay for Doc Makers – the people who create and structure docs. Editors and viewers are completely free. This can be a significant cost advantage for teams where a few power users build docs that many others consume. The Pro plan starts at $10/doc maker/month (billed annually), while the Team plan runs $30/doc maker/month.
Head-to-Head Comparison Pricing Notion charges per user: Plus at $10/user/month, Business at $20/user/month (both billed annually). Every team member who needs access counts as a user.
Coda charges per doc maker: Pro at $10/doc maker/month, Team at $30/doc maker/month (billed annually). Only people who create or edit doc structure pay – everyone else is free.
In practice: A team of 20 where 5 people build docs and 15 just view/fill in data would cost $200/month on Notion Plus but only $50/month on Coda Pro. But if everyone needs to create content, Notion’s per-user pricing can be more predictable.
Features Notion wins on: breadth and polish. The all-in-one workspace experience is smoother, the template gallery is massive (thousands of community templates), and native AI is well-integrated. Project management views (timeline, board, calendar) are more refined. The recent addition of Notion Sites lets you publish pages as simple websites.
Coda wins on: depth and power. The formula system is dramatically more capable, automations can run complex multi-step workflows, and Packs offer deeper integrations than Notion’s native connections. If you want to build a custom CRM, an automated standup bot, or a dynamic reporting dashboard inside a doc, Coda is where you do it.
Ease of Use Notion has a gentler learning curve. The block-based editor is intuitive, and most people can start creating useful pages within minutes. The challenge comes when you try to build complex relational databases – that’s where Notion’s simplicity can become a limitation.
Coda is more powerful but steeper to learn. The formula language takes real investment to master, and building sophisticated docs requires thinking like a developer. The payoff is significant – you can build things in Coda that simply aren’t possible in Notion – but casual users may never need that power.
Who Should Use What? Pick Notion if: you want a single tool for docs, wikis, project management, and light databases. You value a polished UI, a huge template ecosystem, and integrated AI. Your team is mostly creating and organizing content rather than building automated workflows.
Pick Coda if: you have power users who want to build custom workflows, interactive dashboards, or doc-based apps. You benefit from the doc maker billing model (many viewers, few builders). You need deep integrations with external tools and don’t mind a steeper learning curve.
FAQ Can Notion replace Coda (or vice versa)? Partially. Both handle docs and tables well. Notion can’t match Coda’s automation and formula depth. Coda can’t match Notion’s all-in-one workspace polish or AI integration. Many teams use Notion for general workspace needs and Coda for specific data-heavy workflows.
Which has better AI features? Notion, by a significant margin. Notion AI is natively built in, can search across your entire workspace, and handles summarization, writing, and Q&A. Coda has some AI capabilities through Packs, but it’s not as deeply integrated.
Can I migrate from Notion to Coda? Yes. Coda has an official Notion importer that brings over pages and databases. Some manual cleanup is usually needed, especially for complex relational databases. Going the other direction (Coda to Notion) requires more manual work.
Which is better for solo users? Notion. The free plan is generous for individuals, the learning curve is manageable, and the all-in-one approach means fewer tools to juggle. Coda’s power features are overkill for most solo use cases.
Our Recommendation For most teams, Notion is the safer bet. It does 80% of what both tools offer in a more polished, easier-to-adopt package, and the integrated AI features are genuinely useful. The Plus plan at $10/user/month is competitive and covers most needs.
If you have specific, complex workflow needs – custom reporting, automated processes, or docs that need to behave like apps – Coda is the more powerful choice. The doc maker billing model can also make it significantly cheaper for teams with many viewers.
Try Notion free → | Try Coda free →

Quick Verdict Choosing between Semrush and Ahrefs? Here’s the short version: Semrush is the broader marketing platform — SEO, PPC, social media, and content marketing in one dashboard. Ahrefs is the pure SEO powerhouse — the best backlink index in the industry, cleaner interface, and recently more accessible with its new $29/month Starter plan. Pick Semrush for all-in-one marketing. Pick Ahrefs for focused SEO research.
At a Glance Feature Semrush Ahrefs Starting price $139.95/mo (Pro) $29/mo (Starter) Free tier Yes (limited — 10 queries/day) Yes (Webmaster Tools — own site only) Backlink index Large Largest in the industry PPC tools Yes (built-in) No Content marketing Yes (Guru plan+) Limited Best for All-in-one marketing teams SEO specialists and link builders What Is Semrush? Semrush started as a keyword research tool in 2008 and has evolved into the most comprehensive digital marketing platform available. It covers SEO, PPC advertising, social media management, content marketing, and competitive analysis — all from a single subscription.
The platform’s strength is breadth. The Pro plan alone includes keyword research, site auditing, rank tracking (500 keywords), backlink analysis, on-page SEO recommendations, and competitor analysis. Move up to Guru ($249.95/month) and you unlock content marketing tools — a topic research engine, SEO writing assistant, and content audit functionality. The Business plan ($499.95/month) adds API access, share of voice metrics, and white-label reports.
In late 2025, Semrush launched Semrush One, bundling the core SEO toolkit with a new AI Visibility Toolkit designed to track brand mentions and visibility across AI-generated search results. As Google and other search engines increasingly surface AI answers, this forward-looking feature gives Semrush a unique angle that Ahrefs has not yet matched.
What Is Ahrefs? Ahrefs is a Singapore-based SEO toolset known for having the largest backlink index on the web. It crawls over 8 billion pages daily, and its link data is widely considered the most comprehensive and freshest available — making it the go-to tool for link building and backlink analysis.
Beyond backlinks, Ahrefs offers keyword research (Keywords Explorer), rank tracking, site auditing, and content gap analysis. The interface is cleaner and more focused than Semrush’s — it does fewer things, but does them with exceptional depth.
A major shift happened in January 2026 when Ahrefs introduced the Starter plan at $29/month, slashing the entry price by 70%. Previously, the cheapest Ahrefs plan was $99/month (now $129/month as Lite), which priced out freelancers and small site owners. The Starter plan includes 1 project, 100 tracked keywords, and 5K crawl credits per month — enough for a single site or niche blog.
Head-to-Head Comparison Pricing This is where things have shifted significantly in 2026.
Plan Semrush Ahrefs Entry $139.95/mo (Pro) $29/mo (Starter) Mid-tier $249.95/mo (Guru) $129/mo (Lite) Standard — $249/mo (Standard) Advanced $499.95/mo (Business) $449/mo (Advanced) Enterprise Custom $1,499/mo Annual discount ~17% Varies by plan Ahrefs’ new Starter plan has dramatically changed the comparison. At $29/month versus Semrush’s $139.95/month entry point, Ahrefs is now far more accessible for individual site owners. However, the Starter plan is intentionally limited — one project, basic crawl credits, no historical data.
At comparable feature levels, Semrush Pro ($139.95) versus Ahrefs Lite ($129) are close in price, but Semrush includes PPC and content tools that Ahrefs does not offer at any price.
Hidden costs to watch: Ahrefs charges $40-80/month for additional users depending on your plan, and daily rank tracking (vs. weekly) costs an extra $50/month. Semrush includes more user seats in its base plans.
Backlink Analysis Ahrefs wins. Its backlink index is the largest and most frequently updated. The referring domains data, anchor text analysis, and link intersect tool are all best-in-class. If backlink research is your primary use case, there is no better tool.
Semrush has a solid backlink database that has improved significantly in recent years, but it still trails Ahrefs in raw coverage and freshness. For most SEO work, Semrush’s backlink data is sufficient, but link building specialists will notice the difference.
Keyword Research Both tools are excellent here, with slightly different strengths.
Semrush provides keyword difficulty scores, search volume, CPC data, SERP features, and competitive density. Its Keyword Magic Tool is particularly good at expanding seed keywords into large, organized lists. It also shows PPC competition data, which Ahrefs does not.
Ahrefs Keywords Explorer offers similar metrics with the addition of click data — showing not just search volume but estimated clicks, which accounts for zero-click searches. This gives a more realistic picture of traffic potential.
Content and Marketing Tools Semrush wins by a wide margin. The Guru plan includes a full content marketing toolkit: topic research, SEO content templates, a writing assistant that scores content in real time, post tracking, and content auditing. It also offers social media scheduling, PPC research, and advertising analysis.
Ahrefs is laser-focused on SEO. It does not offer PPC tools, social media management, or a built-in content writing assistant. If you need those capabilities, you will need separate tools alongside Ahrefs.
Ease of Use Ahrefs has a cleaner, more intuitive interface. The navigation is straightforward, reports load quickly, and the data presentation is less cluttered. New users typically find Ahrefs easier to learn.
Semrush packs more features into its dashboard, which means more complexity. The learning curve is steeper, and it can feel overwhelming when you first log in. However, once you know where things are, the centralized access to SEO, PPC, and content tools saves time versus switching between platforms.
Who Should Use What? Pick Semrush if: you are a marketing team or agency that needs SEO, PPC research, content marketing, and social media in one platform. The all-in-one approach eliminates the cost and friction of juggling multiple tools. The Pro plan at $139.95/month is expensive but replaces several subscriptions. The new AI Visibility Toolkit also makes it forward-looking for brands concerned about visibility in AI-generated search results.
Pick Ahrefs if: you are an SEO specialist, link builder, or site owner who wants the best backlink data and keyword research without the extra marketing features. The new Starter plan at $29/month makes Ahrefs accessible to freelancers and small site owners for the first time. For pure SEO work, Ahrefs delivers more depth per dollar.
FAQ Is Semrush worth the price? If you use its full feature set — SEO, PPC, content, social — then yes. Semrush replaces multiple tools, which can offset the higher price. If you only need SEO, Ahrefs offers comparable (often better) SEO data at a lower price point.
Can I try Ahrefs for free? Ahrefs Webmaster Tools is free but limited to data about your own verified site. There is no free trial of paid plans. The $29/month Starter plan is the lowest-cost way to access Ahrefs’ full dataset.
Which has better keyword data? Both are excellent. Semrush has a slightly larger keyword database and includes PPC metrics. Ahrefs offers click data that gives more realistic traffic estimates. For most SEO work, either tool provides sufficient keyword intelligence.
Can I switch from one to the other? Both export data to CSV, so you can move keyword lists, backlink reports, and rank tracking data between platforms. However, historical data and custom reports do not transfer — you would be starting fresh with the new tool’s data.
Our Recommendation For solo site owners and freelancers, start with Ahrefs Starter at $29/month. It gives you access to the best backlink index and solid keyword research at a price that was not available a year ago. Upgrade to Lite or Standard as your needs grow.
For marketing teams and agencies, Semrush Pro at $139.95/month is the better investment. The all-in-one platform eliminates tool sprawl, and the content marketing and PPC features justify the premium over Ahrefs. Consider Guru if content optimization is a core workflow.
Try Semrush free → | Try Ahrefs Starter →

Quick Verdict Choosing between Zapier and Make? Here’s the short version: Zapier is the easier, faster option for simple automations – connect two apps, set a trigger, and you’re done. Make (formerly Integromat) is the more powerful and affordable choice for complex, multi-step workflows with branching logic. Zapier trades complexity for speed; Make trades simplicity for control.
At a Glance Feature Zapier Make Free plan 100 tasks/month 1,000 operations/month Paid starting price $19.99/mo (Professional) $10.59/mo (Core) App integrations 7,000+ 2,000+ Workflow builder Linear (step-by-step) Visual (drag-and-drop canvas) Branching logic Paths (paid plans) Built into all plans Error handling Basic Advanced (per-module) AI features Zapier AI + MCP AI agents on all paid plans Best for Simple 2-3 step automations Complex multi-branch workflows What Is Zapier? Zapier is the automation platform that made “if this, then that” accessible to non-technical users. Founded in 2011, it pioneered the concept of connecting web apps without code and remains the most widely used automation tool with over 7,000 app integrations – more than any competitor.
The core experience is building Zaps: automated workflows that trigger when something happens in one app and perform actions in another. “When a new row is added in Google Sheets, create a task in Asana” – that kind of thing. For straightforward automations like these, Zapier is unmatched in ease of use. You can build a working Zap in under five minutes without touching documentation.
Zapier has expanded significantly beyond simple two-step Zaps. Tables and Interfaces let you build lightweight databases and forms directly in Zapier. Paths enable branching logic (if the deal value is over $10K, assign to senior sales; otherwise, route to the team queue). The platform also added Zapier AI for natural-language automation building and MCP (Model Context Protocol) support, letting AI assistants trigger Zaps directly.
Pricing starts with a free plan (100 tasks/month, single-step Zaps only). The Professional plan at $19.99/month (billed annually) includes 750 tasks and multi-step Zaps. The Team plan at $69/month adds shared workspaces, SSO, and collaboration features. Enterprise pricing is custom. Tasks scale up in price as you need more – and this is where Zapier gets expensive fast.
What Is Make? Make (rebranded from Integromat in 2022) is the automation platform for people who think visually and need more control. Instead of Zapier’s linear step-by-step builder, Make uses a visual canvas where you drag modules, draw connections, and build workflows that can branch, loop, and merge in ways that Zapier simply can’t match.
The visual builder is Make’s defining feature. You see your entire automation as a flowchart: data flows between modules, routers split paths based on conditions, and iterators loop through arrays. For complex workflows – say, processing a webhook, checking three different conditions, calling an API, formatting the data, and sending results to different destinations based on the outcome – Make’s canvas makes the logic visible and manageable.
Make is also significantly more affordable than Zapier at scale. The free plan includes 1,000 operations/month (10x Zapier’s free tier). The Core plan at $10.59/month gets you 10,000 operations with unlimited active scenarios, 1-minute minimum run intervals, webhooks, and API access. Pro at $18.82/month adds priority execution and custom variables. Teams at $34.12/month includes team roles, shared templates, and everything in Pro. All paid plans include AI agents and custom AI provider connections (bring your own OpenAI/Anthropic key).
The operations model is more granular than Zapier’s tasks. In Zapier, each action in a Zap counts as one task. In Make, each module that processes data counts as one operation. A 5-step automation in Zapier uses 5 tasks; the same automation in Make uses 5 operations. But Make gives you far more operations per dollar.
Head-to-Head Comparison Pricing This is where Make wins clearly. Let’s compare at similar usage levels:
Usage level Zapier cost Make cost Light (free tier) 100 tasks/mo 1,000 ops/mo Starter paid $19.99/mo (750 tasks) $10.59/mo (10,000 ops) Mid-tier $69/mo (Team, 2,000 tasks) $18.82/mo (10,000 ops + priority) Make gives you roughly 13x more operations per dollar on the entry-level paid plan. As automation volume grows, the gap widens. Zapier’s pricing scales aggressively with task count – heavy users can easily hit $100-300/month.
Features Zapier wins on: app library (7,000+ vs Make’s 2,000+), setup speed for simple automations, built-in Tables and Interfaces, and the sheer breadth of niche app support. If the app you need is obscure, Zapier is more likely to have it.
Make wins on: workflow complexity (branching, looping, error handling), the visual builder, data transformation capabilities, API flexibility (HTTP modules for any REST API), and granular error handling at the module level. Make also includes routers and filters on all plans, including free – Zapier restricts paths to paid plans.
Ease of Use Zapier is easier for beginners. The step-by-step builder guides you through trigger-action pairs with clear prompts. You can build your first automation without any prior knowledge.
Make has a steeper learning curve. The visual canvas is powerful but can be overwhelming at first. Concepts like iterators, aggregators, and routers take time to understand. Once you’re past that curve (give it a weekend), Make becomes faster for building complex workflows than Zapier. But the initial investment is real.
Who Should Use What? Pick Zapier if: you need simple, reliable automations between popular apps and want them running in minutes. You’re a non-technical user who values ease over power. Your use case is mostly straightforward triggers and actions (new lead comes in, send to CRM, notify Slack). You need integrations with niche or industry-specific apps.
Pick Make if: you’re building complex workflows with conditions, loops, and multiple branches. Budget matters – Make gives dramatically more value per dollar. You’re comfortable with a visual builder and willing to invest an afternoon learning it. You need advanced data transformation, API calls, or error handling.
FAQ Is Make really that much cheaper than Zapier? Yes. On entry-level paid plans, Make offers roughly 13x more operations per dollar. The gap narrows slightly at enterprise tiers, but Make remains the more affordable option at every level. The main caveat: if you need a specific app integration that only Zapier has, the price difference is irrelevant.
Can I migrate from Zapier to Make? There’s no one-click migration tool, but the process is manageable. You’ll need to rebuild your automations in Make’s visual builder, which often takes a few hours depending on complexity. Make has equivalents for most Zapier features. Many users report that recreating their Zaps in Make actually improves them, since Make’s builder reveals optimization opportunities.
Which is better for AI workflows? Both are investing heavily in AI. Zapier’s AI can build automations from natural language descriptions and integrates with AI services through its app library. Make includes AI agents on all paid plans and lets you connect your own AI provider keys (OpenAI, Anthropic) on any paid plan. For custom AI pipelines, Make’s visual builder is more flexible.
Do I need to be a developer to use Make? No, but it helps to think systematically. Make’s visual canvas is a no-code tool, but building complex workflows requires understanding concepts like data types, conditional logic, and API basics. If you’ve ever built a complex spreadsheet formula, you can learn Make.
Our Recommendation For quick, simple automations, Zapier is still the fastest path from idea to working automation. The Professional plan at $19.99/month is reasonable for light usage, and the app library is unmatched.
For anything beyond basic trigger-action pairs, Make is the better choice. The Core plan at $10.59/month with 10,000 operations is exceptional value, the visual builder handles complexity that Zapier struggles with, and the per-dollar efficiency means your automation budget goes much further. If you’re willing to spend a weekend learning the interface, Make will serve you better long-term.
